coredata.dc:title: A new Fe/N doped carbon dot naocatalytic amplification-aptamer SERS/RRS/Abs trimode assay platform for ultratrace Pb2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Aihui
Zhang, Ran
Huang, Xiaofang
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Nanocatalytic amplification of carbon dots is a new way to improve sensitivity. The preparation of the high catalytic activity and stable iron/nitrogen-doped carbon dot (CDBFe) sol and its application in aptamer (Apt) assay have not been reported yet. In this paper, a simple hydrothermal procedure for the preparation of CDBFe derived from Fe2+-2,2′-bipyridine complex has been developed. It is found that CDBFe has a strong catalytic effect on the indicator reaction of glyoxal (C2H2O2) reduction of HAuCl4 to produce gold nanoparticle (AuNP) probe with strong surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) at 1617 cm−1, resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) effect at 370 nm and absorption (Abs) at 550 nm. A rapid and sensitive CDBFe catalytic amplification Apt method for SERS/RRS/Abs trimode detecting ultratrace lead ions was established, based on the Apt reaction mediated the nanocatalytic indicator reaction. The results show that the SERS intensity and Pb(II) concentration have a good linear relationship in the range of 1.3–16 pM, and the detection limit is the lowest. In addition, Hg(II) and As(III) can also be measured by this naocatalytic amplification- Apt assay platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe/N doped carbon dots
Nanocatalysis
Aptamer
Trimode molecular spectroscopy
Metal ion detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of FeN-doped carbon dots (CDBFe) catalysts
Molecular spectrum, infrared, XRD, XPS and electron microscopy of CDBFe
SERS spectra of nanocatalysis and aptamer systems

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CDBFe catalyzed gold nanoreaction-Apt SERS/RRS/Abs detection of Pb2+.

Infrared spectrum, XRD, XPS and electron microscopy of CDBFe. A: Infrared spectra of CDBFe, BFe, 2,2′-bipyridine, and ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate. B: XRD patterns of CDBFe, BFe, ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate and 2,2, -bipyridine. C: XPS full spectrum of CDBFe. D: Fe 2p high resolution XPS spectrum of CDBFe. E: O 1 s high resolution XPS spectrum of CDBFe. F: N 1 s high resolution XPS spectrum of CDBFe. G: C 1 s high resolution XPS spectrum of CDBFe. H: TEM of CDBFe.

SERS spectra of CDBFe-C2H2O2-HCl-HAuCl4 catalytic, Apt inhibition and analysis system. A: CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L VBB system, a-g curves represent 0, 16, 32, 60, 72, 128 and 192 μg/L CDBfe, respectively. B: AptPb-128 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L VBB system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.26, 0.53, 0.67, 1 and 2.13 nmol/L AptPb, respectively. C: AptAs-128 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L VBB system, a-g curves represent 0, 0.26, 0.67, 1, 1.2, 1.6 and 2.13 nmol/L AptAs, respectively. D: AptHg-128 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L VBB system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.4, 0.67, 1, 1.2 and 1.6 nmol/L AptHg, respectively. E: Pb(NO3)2–2.13 nmol/L Apt-Pb-128 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.0013, 0.0026, 0.0053, 0.001 and 0.016 nmol/L Pb(NO3)2, respectively. F: NaAsO2-2.13 nmol/L AptAs-128 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L system, a-h curves represent 0, 0.016, 0.026, 0.067, 0.08, 0.1, 0.12 and 0.21 nmol/L NaAsO2, respectively. G: HgCl2-1.6 nmol/L AptHg-128 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-4.67 × 10-4 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-6.67 × 10–7 mol/L system, a-g curves represent 0, 0.0026, 0.0053, 0.016, 0.02, 0.033 and 0.08 nmol/L HgCl2, respectively.

RRS spectra of CDBFe-C2H2O2-HCl-HAuCl4 catalytic, Apt inhibition and analysis system. A: CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 8, 16, 32, 40 and 60 μg/L CDBFe, respectively. B: CDBFe2-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 80, 320, 400, 480 and 600 μg/L CDBFe2, respectively. C: CDBFe3-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 8, 16, 40, 48 and 96 μg/L CDBFe2, respectively. D: CDN-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-g curves represent 0, 8, 16, 32, 48, 60 and 96 μg/L CDN, respectively. E: AptPb-60 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.13, 0.53, 0.667, 0.8 and 1.2 nmol/L AptPb, respectively. F: AptPb-96 μg/L CDN-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-g curves represent 0, 0.267, 0.53, 0.8, 1, 1.2 and 1.6 nmol/L AptPb, respectively. G: AptHg-60 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-g curves represent 0, 0.13, 0.53, 0.8, 1, 1.2 and 1.6 nmol/L AptHg, respectively. H: AptAs-60 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.53, 0.67, 0.8, 1, 1.2 and 1.6 nmol/L AptAs, respectively. I: Pb(NO3)2–1.2 nmol/L Apt-60 μg/L CDBFe-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.0026, 0.0053, 0.008, 0.01 and 0.012 nmol/L Pb(NO3)2, respectively. J: Pb(NO3)2–1.2 nmol/L AptPb-96 μg/L CDN-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2 system, a-f curves represent 0, 0.0013, 0.0053, 0.0067, 0.01 and 0.016 nmol/L Pb(NO3)2, respectively. K: HgCl2-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-60 μg/L CDBFe-1.2 nmol/L AptHg system, a-f curves represented 0, 6.67 × 10-3, 0.013, 0.02, 0.04 and 0.1 nmol/L HgCl2, respectively. L: NaAsO2-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-60 μg/L CDBFe-1.06 nmol/L AptAs system, a-f curves represented 0, 3.33 × 10-3, 6.67 × 10-3, 0.02, 0.033 and 0.067 nmol/L NaAsO2, respectively.

Abs spectra of Apt-CDBFe-HAuCl4-C2H2O2-metal ions system. A: Pb(NO3)2–53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-60 μg/L CDBFe-1.2 nmol/L AptPb system, a-f curves represented 0, 0.003, 0.046, 0.006, 0.0093, 0.013 and 0.016 nmol/L Pb(NO3)2, respectively. B: HgCl2-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-60 μg/L CDBFe-1.2 nmol/L AptHg system, a-f curves represented 0, 0.0099, 0.013, 0.02, 0.06 and 0.08 nmol/L HgCl2, respectively. C: NaAsO2-53.3 μg/mL HAuCl4-1.2 × 10-3 mol/L HCl-3.48 mmol/L C2H2O2-60 μg/L CDBFe-1.06 nmol/L AptAs system, a-g curves represented 0, 0.0099, 0.012, 0.013, 0.02, 0.033 and 0.06 nmol/L NaAsO2, respectively.
Analytical features of the SERS/RRS/Abs Apt assay platform based on HAuCl4-C2H2O2-CDBFe catalytic amplification.

head.ce:title: A new Fe/N doped carbon dot naocatalytic amplification-aptamer SERS/RRS/Abs trimode assay platform for ultratrace Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocatalytic amplification of carbon dots is a new way to improve sensitivity. The preparation of the high catalytic activity and stable iron/nitrogen-doped carbon dot (CDBFe) sol and its application in aptamer (Apt) assay have not been reported yet. In this paper, a simple hydrothermal procedure for the preparation of CDBFe derived from Fe2+-2,2′-bipyridine complex has been developed. It is found that CDBFe has a strong catalytic effect on the indicator reaction of glyoxal (C2H2O2) reduction of HAuCl4 to produce gold nanoparticle (AuNP) probe with strong surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) at 1617 cm−1, resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) effect at 370 nm and absorption (Abs) at 550 nm. A rapid and sensitive CDBFe catalytic amplification Apt method for SERS/RRS/Abs trimode detecting ultratrace lead ions was established, based on the Apt reaction mediated the nanocatalytic indicator reaction. The results show that the SERS intensity and Pb(II) concentration have a good linear relationship in the range of 1.3–16 pM, and the detection limit is the lowest. In addition, Hg(II) and As(III) can also be measured by this naocatalytic amplification- Apt assay platform.

ce:section.ce:para: As the latest form of nano-carbon, carbon dots (CDs) are generally regarded as quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a size below 10 nm [1]. Compared with traditional inorganic quantum dots or noble metal nano-clusters, CDs show better performance and will become a more attractive optical nanoprobe. Its performance is characterized by high chemical stability, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, easier functionalization, and low cost [2]. Therefore, its preparation and its analytical applications have attracted much attention. Current methods for preparing CDs include hydrothermal carbonization, electrochemical oxidation, laser burning, microwave radiation and pyrolysis [3–9]. Among the methods reported, the hydrothermal method is considered to be the simplest, direct and effective method. To improve the fluorescence properties of CDs, we have tried doping different atoms to adjust this property. For example, Fe-doped CDs usually show higher quantum yields. Bourlinos et al. [10] directly synthesized Fe-doped CDs through the oxidative coupling of xylene and anhydrous FeCl3 and proved that they can effectively improve the rate constants of olefin hydrogenation and hydrogen peroxide decomposition as redox nano-catalysts. Zhang [11] et al. proposed a simple preparation method of Fe2O3/CD nanocomposites and studied their effective catalytic activity for the photodegradation of toxic gases. Zhuo et al. [12] obtained iron-doped carbon dots through thermal carbonization of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid salts and ferric nitrate and were used in the detection of dopamine and the application of biological imaging. Fe2+-doped CDs with high fluorescence were synthesized by using proline (Pro) and (NH4)2Fe (SO4)26H2O as precursors by Han et al. [13]. And construct nanohybrid through electrostatic interaction for the detection of Zn2+ and histidine. Yang et al. [14] used L-tartaric acid (TA), urea and FeCl3·6H2O as precursors to synthesize iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe-N-CDs) by microwave, it can catalyze the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2, the Fe-N-CDs exhibited superior catalytic performance over horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and can be used for detection limit as low as 0.1 pg/mL Carcinoembryonic Antigen (CEA). Nitrogen-and iron-containing carbon dots (N, Fe-CDs) are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) and hemin at 180 °C by Wang et al. [15]. The nanodots are catalyzed the oxidation of tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by hydrogen peroxide to form a blue product, which can be used for spectrophotometric and fluorescence determination of 0.05–0.5 μM/0.10–0.65 μM dopamine (DA).

Aptamer (Apt) is a single chain nucleic acid sequence that exhibits high affinity, high selectivity, and stability as a biological probe, which makes it an ideal candidate for the specific recognition of heavy metal ions in biological and environmental samples [16]. Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has become one of the sensitive analysis techniques for organic molecules on nanoparticle surface with Raman peaks [17]. Zhu et al.[18] found that functional chips with 3D organic nanoclusters (3DONs) can lock nucleic acid probes to detect miRNA, and the innovative SERS method can be used to widely distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. To overcome the problem of some metal ions with no Raman effect, some new strategies were developed. Based on the strong catalysis of silver-doped carbon nitride nanoparticles and the aptamer reaction, a SERS and resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) dimode assay was fabricated for ultratrace K+ [19]. RRS is an advanced technology with high sensitivity, convenient performance, and simple equipment, which can be combined with nanoparticles. It is a novel analysis method for biomolecules and metal ions [20]. Qin et al. [21] synthesized glutathione (GSH)-capped CdTe QDs, the interaction between the QDs and albendazole (ABZ) was investigated by RRS spectroscopy to detect albendazole. The detection range was 0–4.752 μg/mL, and the detection limit was 6.5 ng/mL. Li et al. [22] used graphene oxide (GO) to screen the binding with the Apts of L-carnitine chiral enantiomers and was obtained more affinity Apts through RRS technology. As far as we know, there is no report on the preparation of iron and nitrogen-doped carbon dots CDBFe and its Apt RRS for the determination of Pb2+ by the hydrothermal method using Fe2+-2,2′-bipyridine complex as the precursor.

As a wide range of pollutants, heavy metals have potential hazards. Because many heavy metals are non-degradable and continue to exist in the environment, they have serious impacts on the vast majority of organisms and the environment. Pb2+ is a kind of heavy metal ion of environmental pollutants, which poses a serious threat to human health and the environment. Long-term exposure to toxic heavy metals will also cause carcinogenic and teratogenic effects on humans and animals. Excessive Pb2+ may lead to kidney dysfunction and damage brain development, especially in children. In addition, the prosperous development of industry and relatively backward water supply systems are important reasons for Pb pollution, so it is of great significance to detect Pb2+ in the environment and water samples. At present, the commonly used methods for detecting Pb2+ include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [23,24], atomic emission spectrometry (AES) [25], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [26,27], and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [28]. Although these methods have high sensitivity, these analysis techniques and methods require expensive equipment, time-consuming and complicated pretreatment, and professional technicians to operate. In order to solve these problems, some new technologies have been developed rapidly. Zhang et al. [29] proposed a novel fluorescence anisotropy (FA) for the detection of 10 nM-2.0 μM Pb2+ by means of G-quadruplex and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) interaction, with a detection limit was 1.0 nM. Huang et al. [30] reported a colorimetric sensor for rapid detection of Pb2+, which is based on the strategy of DNAzyme-mediated RNA cleavage combined with an annealing-accelerated DNA hybridization chain reaction (HCR). The difference in Pb2+ concentration converts into the difference of DNA assembly, which eventually leads to the color change of colloidal AuNPs, enabling Pb2+ detection to be completed in about 30 min. Li et al. [31] established a Pb2+ driven DNA molecular device, which is used to detect 20 nM-1 μM Pb2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. In this paper, a simple and sensitive SERS/RRS/Abs method for the determination of Pb2+ based on Apt-mediated the nanocatalytic reaction of HAuCl4-glyoxal-CDBFe has been developed. In short, a new iron-doped nitrogen-carbon dot was prepared using Fe2+-2,2′-bipyridine complexes by a hydrothermal method, which is not only strong catalysis, but also inexpensive and simple preparation. Secondly, a new and highly catalysis nanoreaction of CDBFe-glyoxal-HAuCl4 was found and studied by SERS/RRS/Abs techniques. Finally, it was coupled with specific Apt reactions of Pb2+, Hg2+ and As3+, to develop highly sensitive SERS assay and low-cost Abs assay.

SXZ-4-10TC Ceramic Fiber Muffle Furnace (Shanghai, China, power 4000 W, working frequency 50 Hz), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Shanghai, China) scanning to obtain the infrared spectrum. Thermostatic water bath, SYZ-550 quartz sub-boiling distilled water device (Jiangsu, China), ultrasonic cleaner SK 3300B (Shanghai, China), Talos 200S transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). DXR smart Raman spectrometer (Thermo, U.S.). A model of F-7000 Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) is used to scan RRS spectra at volt = 350 V, excited slit = emission slit = 5 nm, and a model of TU-1901 dual-beam ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) were scanned to obtain absorption spectrum.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was bought from Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China, www.guangfu-chem.com). glyoxal, HCl, Lead nitrate, Ferrocene (Fer, AR), Ammonium iron（Ⅱ）sulfate hexahydrate, Mercury dichloride and Sodium arsenite were purchased from Shantou Xilong Science Co., Ltd. (www.xlhg.com, Guangzhou, China), 2,2′-bipyridine were provided by Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (www.macklin.cn, Shanghai, China), Lead ion Apt (AptPb base sequence: 5‘-GGTTGGTGTGGTTGG-3′), the aptamer of As(AptAs base sequence: ATG CAA ACC CTT AAG AAA GTG GTC GTC CAA AAA ACC ATT G), the aptamer of Hg(AptHg base sequence: TTT CTT CTT TCT CCC CTT GTT TGT TGT TT) and Vitoria blue B (VBB) were supplied by Shanghai Shenggong Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (www.sangon.com, Shanghai, China), 0.6 mg/mL CDBFe Stock solution. The reagents used were all analytical pure and the experimental water was ultrapure water.

AgNPs were prepared as follows: 44 mL of sub-boiling water was added to a conical flask and 2 mL of 0.01 mol/L AgNO3, 2 mL of 0.1 mol/L trisodium citrate, 0.6 mL of 10 mol/L H2O2, 0.6 100 mmol/L NaBH4 were added sequentially under stirring conditions and then stirred for 5 min in a 250 °C light-wave oven. The solution was heated for 10 min to obtain an orange-red solution, which was fixed to 50 mL to obtain a concentration of 0.4 mmol/L AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FeN-doped carbon dots (CDBFe) catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Accurately weigh out 0.008 g ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate and 0.01 g 2,2′-bipyridine, then add 30 mL secondary water, transfer to a high-pressure reactor, and place in a muffle furnace after sealing, set the program to 30 min to 180 °C after holding for 2 h. After the reaction, ice water was cooled to room temperature to obtain 0.6 mg/mL iron and nitrogen-doped carbon dots CDBFe for standby. Comparing the catalytic effects of CDBFe non-dialysis and dialysis, the non-dialysis CDBFe catalysis effect is stronger, higher sensitivity. Therefore, the non-dialysis CDBFe are selected to complete the following experiments. Accurately weigh out 0.008 g ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate and 0.01 g 2,2′-bipyridine, then added 30 mL distilled water to the digester. The concentration of 0.6 mg/ ml CDBFe3 was obtained by the above microwave procedure. Accurately weigh out 0.018 g 2,2′-bipyridine, then add 30 mL secondary water, transfer to a high-pressure reactor, and place in a muffle furnace after sealing, set the program to 30 min to 180 °C after holding for 2 h. After the reaction, ice water was cooled to room temperature to obtain 0.6 mg/mL nitrogen-doped carbon dots CDN.

In a 5.0 mL graduated test tube, 320 μL 10 nmol/L Apt, 20–240 μL 0.1 nmol/L lead ion and 320 μL 0.6 mg/L CDBFe were transferred to the water bath at 85℃ for 5 min, and then 60 μL 0.087 mol/L C2H2O2, 70 μL 0.01 mol/L HCl and 80 μL with a concentration of 1 mg/mL (0.1%) HAuCl4 was added, dilute to 1.5 mL, and mixed. After 35 min in a water bath at 85 °C, the reaction was terminated with ice water. Then add 100 μL of 10 μmol/L VBB and mix well, take the solution in a quartz dish. The power of the laser was kept at 5.0 mW and the acquisition time was 5 s and the SERS peak at 1617 cm−1 (I1617 cm-1) and a blank without adding lead ion (I1617 cm-1)0 were obtained by DXR smart Raman spectrometer, to calculate ΔI 1617 cm-1 = I 1617 cm-1-(I 1617 cm-1)0. For the RRS assay, no VBB probes were added.

The stable Fe2+-2,2′-bipyridine complex was formed by the precursors ammonium iron（Ⅱ）sulfate hexahydrate and 2,2′-bipyridine. The complex was heated at 180℃ for 2 h and hydrothermal reaction to generate iron and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDBFe). These carbon dots have a strong catalytic effect on the reduction of chloroauric acid by C2H2O2 to AuNPs. When lead ion Apt is added, it will adsorb and wrap CDBFe, which will weaken the catalysis effect of CDBFe. When the target molecule Pb2+ is added, CDBFe will be released after specific binding with Apt, and the catalytic activity of CDBFe will be restored. As the concentration of Pb2+ gradually increases, the amount of CDBFe desorbed also increases, and the concentration of AuNPs as an indicator also increases, thereby the SERS/RRS/Abs signal increase linearly (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular spectrum, infrared, XRD, XPS and electron microscopy of CDBFe
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 A-C shows that there is no regular fluorescence signal of 2,2′-bipyridine, ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate and BFe (mixture of 2,2′-bipyridine and ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate). The fluorescence peak of CDBFe is located at 420 nm (Fig. S1 D, Volt = 500 V, excited slit = emission slit = 10 nm). In the concentration range of 0–0.16 g/L, the fluorescence intensity F also has a good linear relationship with the concentration. The Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) spectrum obtained by using fluorometer synchronous scanning technology is a fast and sensitive technique for studying the scattering characteristics of nanoparticles. First, we scanned the RRS spectra of 2,2, -bipyridine, ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate and BFe (Fig. S1 E-G), and they all exist RRS signal but irregular. For the RRS spectra of CDBFe (volt = 350 V, excited slit = emission slit = 10 nm), the RRS intensity at 370 nm showed a linear correlation with the CDBFe concentration (Fig. S1 H). Similarly, the Abs spectra of 2,2′-bipyridine and ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate are irregular (Fig. S1 I and J), but BFe shows extremely regular signals at 300–600 nm, with two absorption peaks at 425 nm and 350 nm, and the absorbance increases with increasing concentration (Fig. S1 K). Fig. S1 L shows that CDBFe has a broad absorption band at 250–600 nm, and its absorbance gradually increases with the increase of CDBFe concentration, the CDBFe concentration, and the Abs intensity have good linearity within a certain concentration range.

When studying the SERS spectrum of CDBFe with AgNPs as the SERS substrate, it was found that there was a Raman signal in CDBFe itself, but there is no regularity (Fig. S1 M). The infrared spectrum shows that CDBFe has 8 characteristic peaks (Fig. 2 A), of which 489.4 cm−1 is caused by C-H stretching vibration. 767.1 cm−1 should retain the peak of 2,2′-bipyridine 757.65 cm−1, 1022.8 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H in-plane deformation of benzene compounds, and 1157.2 cm−1 is caused by the single bond vibration of the ring. 1441.8 cm−1 is attributed to C = C stretching vibration, 1602.4 cm−1 is similar to the peaks in BFe and ammonium iron(Ⅱ)sulfate hexahydrate which may be caused by the stretching vibration of olefin compound C = C. 3079.5 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H stretching vibration of cyclopentadiene. 3221.0 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of C-OH, or it may be caused by the presence of crystal water in the test sample.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CDBFe, BFe, 2,2′-bipyridine and ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate were studied, and the XRD analysis results showed (Fig. 2 B), 2,2′-bipyridine, ammonium iron(Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate and BFe can all be clearly seen as the crystal structure. However, the CDBFe generated by hydrothermal treatment of BFe has a characteristic peak at 20-30°, which is the peak of graphite carbon (002), indicating that CDBFe has an amorphous carbon structure. Meanwhile, the phase of iron and iron compounds cannot be seen visible in XRD, indicating that there are no obvious Fe particles in CDBFe.

XPS is used to analyze the composition and surface state of particles. Fig. 2 C is the XPS full spectrum of CDBFe, which shows that CDBFe contains four elements: C, O, Fe, and N, with relative atomic percentages of 55.81%, 38.2%, 0.69% and 5.3%. Among them, Fig. 2 G is the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s. There are element characteristic peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 289.9 eV, corresponding to six types of carbon bonds, C-C (sp2), C-O-C and C = O, respectively. The peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s are 400.0, 401.5, and 402.4 eV, which are attributed to C-N, N-(C)3 and N-H bonds, respectively. The characteristic peaks of the three elements in the Fe2p high-resolution spectrum are at 708.5, 711.9 and 721.2 eV respectively. The peak at 708.5 eV is Fe 2p3/2, 711.9 eV is a satellite peak associated with Fe 2p3/2, and the peak at 724.7 eV corresponds to Fe 2p1/2 [32]. The presence of these three peaks also confirms the presence of Fe3+. Fig. 2 H is the TEM image of CDBFe at 50 nm and 10 nm resolution, with a particle size of about 3 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS spectra of nanocatalysis and aptamer systems
ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of HAuCl4 by glyoxal (C2H2O2) is difficult to form AuNPs. CDBFe has the characteristics of carbon dots and iron atoms with abundant surface electrons that enhanced the redox electron transfer between glyoxal and Au3+ to produce Au0 atoms that grow into AuNPs. Thus, it exhibits excellent catalytic performance. The produced AuNPs exhibit SERS signals when VBB molecular probes were added. The system produces three SERS peaks at 1201 cm−1, 1395 cm−1 and 1617 cm−1, of which the SERS peak at 1617 cm−1 The signal is the strongest, and with the increase of CDBFe concentration, its SERS intensity gradually increases. For the CDBFe-Apt-HAuCl4-glyoxal reaction, when the target Pb2+ is added, it will specifically bind to Aptpb, thereby releasing CDBFe and restoring its catalytic activity, and increasing the target concentration will release more CDBFe. Then more gold nanoparticles will be generated. After adding the molecular probe VBB, within a certain linear range, the signal intensity of the SERS spectrum will increase with the increase of the target concentration (Fig. 3 E). At the same time, this method can also detect As3+ and Hg2+ (Fig. 3F and 3G).

It is difficult for the reaction between C2H2O2 and HAuCl4 at room temperature, but in the water bath at 80℃, CDBFe can catalyze the reaction of C2H2O2-HAuCl4 to form pink-purple AuNPs. AuNPs have three RRS peaks at 280 nm, 370 nm, and 550 nm, respectively. Among them, the RRS peak is the strongest at 370 nm, and there is a linear relationship between the RRS intensity and the CDBFe concentration. Then 10 mL CDBFe was dialyzed with an intercalated molecular weight of 3500 da dialysis bag for 12 h. A concentration of 0.6 mg/mL of dialysis CDBFe2 was obtained. Compared with the non-dialyzed and dialyzed CDBFe catalysis, it was found that the sensitivity of non-dialyzed was better (Fig. 4 A and 4B). CDBFe was prepared by microwave digestion apparatus to obtain CDBFe3. The system was heated for 35 min at 80 °C in the water bath, and CDBFe3 was used as a catalyst to catalyze the C2H2O2-HAuCl4 system. The results showed that different CDBFe can catalyze the system. Purplish red AuNPs were finally produced. The RRS strength of AuNPs at 370 nm was linearly increasing with the amount of CDBFe. Among them, CDBFe had the best catalytic effect and the most sensitive reaction. Experiments show that Apt can inhibit the catalytic reaction of CDBFe-Apt-HAuCl4-C2H2O2, and the RRS intensity decreased with Apt increasing (Fig. 4E-4H). When the target Pb2+ is added, CDBFe or CDN will be released, thereby restoring the catalytic activity. In order to prove the better effect of Fe/N-doped CDs, we try to compare with nitrogen-doped carbon dots CDN. The results prove that CDBFe has better sensitivity to this system than CDN (Fig. 4I and 4 J). For the other target ions such as Hg2+and As3+, this CDBFe catalytic amplification RRS assay platform can be also used to determine (Fig. 4K and 4L). For the target Cd2+, the addition of 1 nm 280 μL of AptCd could inhibit the catalytic activity of CDBFe, but the addition of the analyte Cd2+ could not restore the catalytic activity of CDBFe. AuNPs cannot be generated, so Cd2+ cannot be detected.

According to the section of the experimental method, the Apt-CDBFe-C2H2O2-HCl-HAuCl4-Pb2+ analysis system was scanned by ultraviolet absorption spectrum, and an obvious absorption peak was generated at 550 nm ascribing to AuNPs surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption peak. With the increase of Pb2+ content, more AuNPs were produced, and the absorption spectra of the system became stronger gradually (Fig. 5 A). At the same time, when detecting other targets, such as Hg2+and As3+, this CDBFe catalytic amplification Abs assay platform can be also used to determine (Fig. 5B and 5C). Although the Abs method can be used to determine Pb2+, Hg2+and As3+, the slope of the working curve was very smaller than the RRS, that is, the sensitivity and accuracy were inferior to the RRS method.

According to the experimental method of 2.4, 5 μL solution was repeatedly added to the ultra-thin carbon film until the ultra-thin carbon film could be found black spots under the optical microscope, and then the transmission electron microscope (TEM) was recorded. For the Apt-CDBFe-C2H2O2-HCl-HAuCl4-Pb2+ system, when there is no Pb2+ in the solution (Fig. S2 A), fewer AuNPs are formed and the average particle size is about 50 nm. When a certain amount of Pb2+ was added to the solution (Fig. S2 B), CDBFe was gradually released, the catalytic effect of the system was gradually enhanced, the number of AuNPs generated by the reaction increased, and the average particle size was 30 nm, with obvious aggregation phenomenon. It can be seen from the EDS graph that the produced gold nanoparticles have strong Au-M characteristic peaks at 2.1 KeV, relatively a strong Au-Lα peak at 9.7 KeV, and also have obvious Lβ1 and Lβ2 characteristic peaks at 11.4 keV and 11.6 keV. There is also a weak Au-Lγ characteristic peak at 13.4 keV. These characteristic peaks are enough to indicate the formation of gold nanoparticles in the system.

Under experimental conditions, both CDBFe and CDN can catalyze the reduction of HAuCl4 by C2H2O2 to form AuNPs, and the characteristics are shown in Table S1. The results show that within a certain concentration range, the more CDBFe is added, the stronger the catalytic ability. The slope of CDBFe prepared by the hydrothermal method is larger than that of CDBFe3 prepared by the microwave digestion instrument, that is, the sensitivity is higher and the linearity is better. CDBFe has a stronger catalytic effect than CDN. This is because the former contains electron-rich Fe atoms, which have more surface electrons and can accelerate the electrons transfer of C2H2O2-HAuCl4 redox reaction and generate more AuNPs (Fig. 6 ). When the Apt is added to the system, it will encase the catalyst and block the contact with the reactants, thereby inhibiting its catalytic effect, and the RRS signal will also decrease. The system ΔI370nm has a negative correlation with the concentration of Apt.

The influence of reagent concentration on the determination was studied. In the RRS system, when the concentration of CDBFe is 0.06 mg/L (Fig. S3 A), the concentration of AptPb is 1.2 nM (Fig. S3 B), and the concentration of glyoxal solution is 3.48 mmol/L (Fig. S3 C). The concentration of auric chloride is 53.3 μg/mL (Fig. S3 D), the concentration of HCl is 1.2 × 10-3 mol/L (Fig. S3 E), the temperature is 80 °C (Fig. S3 F), and the time is 35 min (Fig. S3 G). Therefore, the above conditions are the best reaction conditions. The other Apt concentration was 1.2 nM AptHg, and 1.06 nM AptAs (Fig. S3 H and I). For the SERS system, we optimized the relevant conditions. When the concentration of AptPb and AptAs reached 2.13 nmol/L (Fig. S3 J and K), the concentration of AptHg was 1.6 nmol/L (Fig. S3 L), the concentration of HCl was 4.67 × 10-4 mol/L (Fig. S3 M), and the concentration of CDBFe was 128 μg/L (Fig. S3 N), the temperature is 85 °C (Fig. S3 O). When the above conditions are reached, the reaction system reaches the optimal conditions.

The interference of coexisting substances on the detection of Pb2+ was investigated according to the experimental method. The relative error is within ± 10% when the concentration of Pb2+ is 0.013 nmol/L. 1000 times of Na+ and Ca2+, 500 times of K+, 100 times of Fe2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, NO2–, Al3+, HPO4 2-, As3+, 50 times of Cr3+, HCO3–, Zn2+, H2PO4 -, P2O7 4-, Cu2+, Hg2+, and 10 times of CO3 2– and HSA have no interference in the determination of Pb2+ (Table S3).

A 50 mL of domestic wastewater, tap water, drinking water, river water, and rainwater were obtained from Yanshan Campus of Guangxi Normal University, and 50 mL of medical wastewater was obtained from Yong'an Health Hospital. After centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 r/min, then filtered and diluted to a certain multiple for reserve[33,34]. According to the experimental method for Pb2+ detection, the recovery rate was between 91.7 and 103.8%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was between 3.1 and 8.5% (Table S4).

In this paper, a new type of iron/nitrogen-doped carbon dots was prepared by hydrothermal method using Fe(II)-2,2′-bipyridine complex as precursors. The results show that CDBFe has a strong catalytic activity for the reduction of HAuCl4 with glyoxal to form gold nanoparticles with surface plasmon SERS, RRS and absorption effects. When the Apt coated CDBFe, the catalytic activity of CDBFe would be very weak. After adding the corresponding target Pb2+, CDBFe is exposed again and its catalytic activity is restored. With the increase of the concentration of the target, the number of gold nanoparticles generated also increases, which makes the SERS/RRS/Abs signal linearly enhanced. Based on this, a new Apt SERS/RRS/Abs trimode method for rapid and sensitive detection of Pb2+ was established and this method has extremely high sensitivity and very low detection limit. The method was applied to the determination of Pb2+ in actual water samples, and the recovery rate is between 91.7 ∼ 103.8%. in addition, this new Apt SERS/RRS/Abs assay platform can be used to detect other harmful elements such as As and Hg.

Aihui Liang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ran Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaofang Huang: Visualization, Investigation. Zhiliang Jiang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
